topic: A person finds out their partner has been learning their native language
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a subject line so innocuous—"New Language App Suggestion"—that I almost ignored it. But when I clicked it, the message wasn't a suggestion; it was a transcript. A full, grammatically complex conversation, peppered with idioms and nuanced phrasing, all in my mother tongue. My hands started shaking so violently I had to grip the desk edge to keep them from flying. I reread the final sentence, my breath catching in my throat. It was flawless. My partner, Mark, had been studying my language. The realization hit me like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the living room, my footsteps loud and uneven on the hardwood floor. I kept running my hands through my hair, a frantic, repetitive motion. "You... you were doing this?" The words came out thin and reedy, sounding utterly foreign even to my own ears. The silence in the apartment amplified the frantic drumming inside my chest.

[story 2]

I found the proof tucked inside a textbook Mark had left on the counter. It wasn't a note; it was a series of meticulously corrected sentences, exercises I had struggled with for years, now rendered perfectly. A wave of nausea washed over me, and I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to scrub the images away. My vision swam. I couldn't focus on anything—the pattern of the wallpaper, the way the afternoon light slanted across the floor. I walked into the kitchen, my movements jerky, and stared at the screen where the text glowed. My jaw tightened until my teeth ached. I let out a short, sharp sound, half-gasp, half-choke, and backed away until the cool surface of the refrigerator pressed against my spine. Every nerve ending felt exposed, buzzing with a chaotic energy that made my skin crawl. I needed to scream, but my throat was constricted, producing only a dry, rattling sound.

[story 3]

When I saw the vocabulary list, the carefully organized structure of the conjugation tables, the blood seemed to drain from my face. It wasn't just studying; it was immersion. He had been building a whole world in my language, quietly, behind my back. A dizzying vertigo set in; the room tilted slightly, and I had to steady myself against the doorframe. I started whispering, testing the air, trying to hear the sound of my own voice, but it felt alien. My fingers began to pick at the fabric of my sweater, tearing at the threads in a desperate attempt to disrupt the overwhelming sensory input. I couldn't articulate anything coherent. I just stood there, rigid, my whole body vibrating with an intense, pressurized feeling, unable to move toward comfort or away from the stark, terrifying truth staring back at me from the screen.
